nodes.title: SuperCommAlgebra
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Section 2.1.}
  A \(\mathbb{Z}\)-graded unital \(\mathbb{C}\)-algebra \(D_\bullet\) is \emph{supercommutative}
  if multiplication satisfies
  \[v \cdot w = (-1)^{|v||w|} w \cdot v,\]
  where \(|v| \in \{0,1\}\) is the parity of \(v\), defined by \(|v| \equiv \deg(v) \bmod 2\).

  A \emph{superderivation of degree \(r\)} on \(D_\bullet\) is a graded \(\mathbb{C}\)-linear map
  \(R \colon D_\bullet \to D_{\bullet+r}\) satisfying the graded Leibniz rule:
  \[R(v \cdot w) = R(v) \cdot w + (-1)^{r|v|}\, v \cdot R(w).\]

nodes.title: FreeSuperCommAlgebra
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Definitions 2.1 and 2.3.}
  For a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-graded \(\mathbb{C}\)-vector space \(C_\bullet\), let
  \(\SSym[C_\bullet]\) denote the unital supercommutative algebra freely generated
  by \(C_\bullet\). For \(C^\bullet\) the graded dual of \(C_\bullet\), define the
  dual completion:
  \[D^\bullet \coloneqq \SSym\llbracket C^\bullet \rrbracket
    = \prod_{i \geq 0} \SSym[C^\bullet]^i.\]
  Given any degree-\(r\) linear map \(f \colon C_\bullet \to B_{\bullet+r}\), there
  is a unique algebra homomorphism \(\SSym\llbracket f \rrbracket\) and a unique
  derivation \(\mathrm{Der}(f)\) of degree \(r\) extending \(f\).

  The pairing \(\langle -,- \rangle \colon C^\bullet \times C_\bullet \to \mathbb{C}\)
  extends to a cap product
  \(\cap \colon D^\bullet \times D_\bullet \to D_\bullet\)
  by requiring that \(\nu \cap (-)\) for \(\nu \in C^\bullet\) acts as a
  superderivation of degree \(-\deg(\nu)\) restricting to
  \(\langle \nu, - \rangle\). This makes \(D_\bullet\) a left \(D^\bullet\)-module.

nodes.title: HolDescendentSpace
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Definition 2.5.}
  Let \(X\) be a smooth projective variety over \(\mathbb{C}\). Define
  \(\mathrm{CH}^X\) as the \(\mathbb{C}\)-vector space generated by symbols
  \[\chh_i(\gamma) \quad \text{for } i \geq 0,\ \gamma \in H^\bullet(X),\]
  subject to linearity in \(\gamma\). The cohomological \(\mathbb{Z}\)-grading is
  \[\deg \chh_i(\gamma) = 2i - p + q \quad \text{for } \gamma \in H^{p,q}(X).\]

nodes.title: DescendentAlgebra
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Definition 2.5.}
  The \emph{algebra of holomorphic descendents} is
  \[\BD^X \coloneqq \SSym\llbracket \mathrm{CH}^X \rrbracket,\]
  the degree-completion of the free supercommutative algebra on \(\mathrm{CH}^X\).
  Write \(\chh_\bullet(\gamma) \coloneqq \sum_{i \geq 0} \chh_i(\gamma) \in \BD^X\).

nodes.title: TopDescendentAlgebra
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Definition 2.7.}
  For a topological type \(\alpha \in K^0_\sst(X)\), let
  \(\widetilde{\mathrm{CH}}^X_\alpha\) be generated by symbols
  \(\mathrm{ch}_i(\gamma)\) for \(i \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}\), \(\gamma \in H^\bullet(X)\),
  and set \(\BD^X_\alpha \coloneqq \SSym\llbracket \widetilde{\mathrm{CH}}^X_\alpha \rrbracket\).
  There is an algebra homomorphism \(p_\alpha \colon \BD^X \to \BD^X_\alpha\) sending
  \[\chh_i(\gamma) \mapsto \begin{cases}
    \mathrm{ch}_{i - \lfloor (p-q)/2 \rfloor}(\gamma)
      & \text{if } \deg \chh_i(\gamma) > 0, \\
    \int_X \gamma \cdot \mathrm{ch}(\alpha)
      & \text{if } \deg \chh_i(\gamma) = 0, \\
    0 & \text{otherwise.}
  \end{cases}\]

nodes.title: VirasoroOpR
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Section 2.3, item (1).}
  For \(k \geq -1\), the operator \(\bR_k \colon \BD^X \to \BD^X\) is the even
  superderivation of degree \(2k\) defined on generators by
  \[\bR_k\bigl(\chh_i(\gamma)\bigr) \coloneqq
    \Bigl(\prod_{j=0}^k (i+j)\Bigr)\, \chh_{i+k}(\gamma),\]
  with the convention that the product is \(1\) when \(k = -1\), and
  \(\chh_{i+k}(\gamma) = 0\) if \(i+k < 0\).

nodes.title: VirasoroOpT
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Section 2.3, item (2).}
  For \(k \geq -1\), the operator \(\bT_k \colon \BD^X \to \BD^X\) is
  multiplication by the element
  \[\bT_k \coloneqq \sum_{i+j=k} (-1)^{\dim X - p^L}\, i!\, j!\,
    \chh_i \chh_j(\tdX) \in \BD^X,\]
  where \(\chh_i \chh_j(\tdX) \coloneqq \sum_t (-1)^{\dim X - p_t^L}
  \chh_i(\gamma_t^L)\, \chh_j(\gamma_t^R)\) for the Künneth decomposition
  \(\Delta_* \tdX = \sum_t \gamma_t^L \otimes \gamma_t^R\) with
  \(\gamma_t^L \in H^{p_t^L, q_t^L}(X)\).

nodes.title: VirasoroOp
VirasoroBracket
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Remark 2.12.}
  The operators \(\{\bL_k\}_{k \geq -1}\) satisfy the Virasoro commutator relations
  \[[\bL_k, \bL_\ell] = (\ell - k)\, \bL_{k+\ell} \in \mathrm{End}(\BD^X),\]
  where \([X,Y] = X \circ Y - Y \circ X\) is the commutator.
nodes.proof_tex:
\uses{def:virasoro-R, def:virasoro-T}
  Direct computation on generators; see van Bree, Proposition~2.10. The three sub-cases are:
  \begin{enumerate}
  \item[(a)] \([\bR_k, \bR_\ell](\chh_i(\gamma)) = (\ell - k)\bR_{k+\ell}(\chh_i(\gamma))\):
        by substituting the explicit product formula \(\bR_k(\chh_i(\gamma)) = \bigl(\prod_{j=0}^k(i+j)\bigr)\chh_{i+k}(\gamma)\)
        and computing the iterated application \(\bR_k \bR_\ell - \bR_\ell \bR_k\) on generators;
        the result follows from a combinatorial identity on rising factorials.
  \item[(b)] Mixed term \([\bR_k, \bT_\ell]\): since \(\bT_\ell\) is multiplication by
        an element of \(\BD^X\) and \(\bR_k\) is a derivation, the commutator
        \([\bR_k, \bT_\ell](u) = \bR_k(\bT_\ell) \cdot u\) (derivation applied to the
        multiplication element); a direct computation shows \(\bR_k(\bT_\ell) = (\ell - k)\bT_{k+\ell}\)
        up to the correct sign.
  \item[(c)] \([\bT_k, \bT_\ell] = 0\): both \(\bT_k\) and \(\bT_\ell\) are multiplication
        operators on the commutative algebra \(\BD^X\), so their commutator vanishes.
  \end{enumerate}
  Sorry in Lean.

nodes.title: WeightZeroDescendents
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Definition 2.13.}
  The \emph{algebra of weight-0 descendents} is
  \[\BD^X_\inv \coloneqq \{D \in \BD^X \mid \bR_{-1}(D) = 0\}.\]
  Similarly, for a topological type \(\alpha \in K^0_\sst(X)\):
  \[\BD^X_{\inv,\alpha} \coloneqq \{D \in \BD^X_\alpha \mid \bR_{-1}(D) = 0\}.\]
  The descendent integrals \(\int_{[M]^\vir} D\) are independent of the choice of
  universal sheaf for \(D \in \BD^X_\inv\).

nodes.title: WeightZeroVirasoroOp
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Definition 2.14.}
  The \emph{weight-0 Virasoro operator} \(\Linv \colon \BD^X \to \BD^X_\inv\) is
  \[\Linv \coloneqq \sum_{j \geq -1} \frac{(-1)^j}{(j+1)!}\, \bL_j \circ \bR_{-1}^{j+1}.\]
  That \(\Linv\) maps into \(\BD^X_\inv\) follows from the identity
  \([\bR_{-1}, \bL_j] = (j+1)\bL_{j-1}\), which gives \(\bR_{-1} \circ \Linv = 0\).

nodes.title: VirasoroConjectureSheavesInv
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Conjecture 2.15.}
  Let \(M\) be a moduli space of semistable sheaves on \(X\) with virtual fundamental
  class \([M]^\vir\). Then
  \[\int_{[M]^\vir} \Linv(D) = 0 \quad \text{for all } D \in \BD^X.\]

nodes.title: VirasoroConjectureNormalized
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Section 2.4 (the \(\delta\)-normalized
  Virasoro conjecture).}
  Let \(M = M_\alpha\) be a moduli space of sheaves of topological type \(\alpha\) and
  let \(\delta \in H^\bullet(X, \mathbb{Z})\) be an algebraic class with
  \(\int_X \delta \cdot \mathrm{ch}(\alpha) \neq 0\). A universal sheaf \(\BG\) is
  \emph{\(\delta\)-normalized} if \(\xi_\BG(\chh_1(\delta)) = 0\). Define the
  \(\delta\)-normalized Virasoro operators (for \(k \geq -1\))
  \[\bL_k^\delta \coloneqq \bR_k + \bT_k + \bS_k^\delta, \qquad
    \bS_k^\delta \coloneqq -\frac{(k+1)!}{\int_X \delta \cdot \mathrm{ch}(\alpha)}\,
    \bR_{-1} \circ \chh_{k+1}(\delta).\]
  Then for a \(\delta\)-normalized universal sheaf \(\BG\):
  \[\int_{[M]^\vir} \xi_\BG\bigl(\bL_k^\delta(D)\bigr) = 0
    \quad \text{for all } k \geq -1,\ D \in \BD^X.\]

nodes.title: NormalizedEquivalentToInvariant
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Section 2.4, Proposition (normalized vs.\
  invariant).}
  \cref{conj:virasoro-sheaves-inv} and \cref{conj:normalizedVirasoro} are equivalent.

  \textbf{Proof}: Sorry. This is the equivalence proposition in Section 2.4 of the paper.
nodes.proof_tex:
Sorry in Lean. The identity
  \(\Linv = \sum_{j \geq -1} \tfrac{(-1)^j}{(j+1)!} \bL_j^\delta \bR_{-1}^{j+1}\)
  (the \(\bS^\delta\)-terms cancel) shows that the normalized conjecture implies the
  invariant one. The reverse implication is a backward induction on \(k\): the case
  \(k > \mathrm{virdim}(M)\) is clear by degree, and applying \(\Linv\) to
  \(F^{k+1}D\) with \(F = \chh_1(\delta)\) (so \(\xi_\BG(F) = 0\)) isolates the
  \(\bL_k^\delta\) term using the induction hypothesis.

nodes.title: PairDescendentAlgebra
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Section 2.5.}
  For a sheaf \(V\) on \(X\), the \emph{algebra of pair descendents} is
  \(\Dpa \coloneqq \BD^X \otimes \BD^X\),
  where generators of the first factor are denoted \(\chh_i^V(\gamma)\) (for the
  sheaf \(V\)) and generators of the second factor \(\chh_i^F(\gamma)\) (for the
  varying sheaf \(F\)).

nodes.title: PairVirasoroOp
VirasoroConjecturePairs
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Conjecture 2.16.}
  Let \(P\) be a moduli space of pairs with universal pair \(q^*V \to \mathbb{F}\)
  on \(P \times X\). Then
  \[\int_{[P]^\vir} \xi_\mathbb{F}\bigl(\bL_k^V(D)\bigr) = 0
    \quad \text{for all } k \geq 0,\, D \in \BD^X.\]
  Equivalently:
  \[\int_{[P]^\vir} \xi_{(q^*V, \mathbb{F})}\bigl(\bL_k^\pa(D)\bigr) = 0
    \quad \text{for all } k \geq 0,\, D \in \Dpa.\]

nodes.title: VertexAlgebra
ConformalElement
PrimaryStates
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Section 3.4 (following Borcherds and Kac).}
  Let \((V_\bullet, \omega)\) be a VOA. The space of \emph{primary states of
  conformal weight \(i\)} is
  \[P_i \coloneqq \{a \in V_i^\omega \mid L_n(a) = 0\ \text{for all } n \geq 1\}.\]
  The associated Lie algebra quotient is
  \(\widecheck{V}_\bullet = V_{\bullet+2} / T V_\bullet\) with Borcherds' Lie bracket
  \([\bar{a}, \bar{b}] = \overline{a_{(0)} b}\), and the Lie algebra of primary states
  is \(\widecheck{P}_0 = P_1 / T(P_0)\).

nodes.title: PrimaryStatesEqualKernel
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Section 3.4, Proposition (primary states vs.\
  the \([-,\omega]\)-kernel).}
  For a conformal vertex algebra \((V_\bullet, \omega)\), define the Lie subalgebra
  \[\widecheck{K}_0 \coloneqq \{\bar{a} \in \widecheck{V}_0^\omega \mid [\bar{a}, \omega] = 0\}
    \subseteq \widecheck{V}_0^\omega.\]
  In general \(\widecheck{P}_0 \subseteq \widecheck{K}_0\) (from
  \([-,\omega] = \sum_{n \geq -1} \tfrac{(-1)^n}{(n+1)!} T^{n+1} \circ L_n\)).
  Conversely, if \((V_\bullet, \omega)\) is a lattice vertex operator algebra with
  \(V_\bullet = \mathbb{C}[\Lambda_\sst] \otimes \BD_\Lambda\), then
  \(\widecheck{P}_0 = \widecheck{K}_0\) on the summands \(e^\alpha \otimes \BD_\Lambda\)
  for which \(\alpha\) is not torsion.

  \textbf{Proof}: Sorry. This is the Section~3.4 primary-states proposition of the paper.
nodes.proof_tex:
Sorry in Lean. Given a non-torsion \(\alpha\) and \(\bar{a} \in \widecheck{K}_0\)
  with lift \(a \in e^\alpha \otimes \BD_\Lambda\) (so \(a_{(0)}\omega = 0\)), choose
  \(b \in \Lambda\) with \(Q(\alpha, b) = 1\) by non-degeneracy of \(Q\). Then
  \(\eta_b(\bar{a}) \coloneqq -a_{(0)}b\) is another lift of \(\bar{a}\) lying in
  \(P_1\): the identity
  \(\omega_{(n+1)}(a_{(0)}b) = a_{(0)}(\omega_{(n+1)}b) - (a_{(0)}\omega)_{(n+1)}b = 0\)
  for \(n \geq 1\) uses \(a_{(0)}\omega = 0\) and \(b \in P_1\).

nodes.title: WallCrossingCompatibility
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Propositions 4.5 and 4.6.}
  The subspace of primary states \(\widecheck{P}_0 \subseteq \widecheck{V}_\bullet\)
  is a Lie subalgebra, and \(P_0^\pa \subseteq V_\bullet^\pa\) is a Lie submodule
  over \(\widecheck{P}_0\). In particular, if \([M_1]^\inva\) and \([M_2]^\inva\)
  are primary states, then so is any class appearing in their Lie bracket in Joyce's
  wall-crossing formula: the Virasoro constraints are preserved under wall-crossing.

  \textbf{Proof}: Sorry. This is Propositions~4.5 and~4.6 of the paper.
nodes.proof_tex:
\uses{def:primary-states}
  Sorry in Lean. For the Lie subalgebra claim: if \(a, b \in P_1\), then
  \(L_n(a_{(0)} b) = 0\) for all \(n \geq 1\), because primary states commute with
  all Virasoro operators \(L_n\) (Borcherds). For the submodule claim: the same
  argument shows that if \(a \in P_1^\pa\) and \(\bar{c} \in \widecheck{P}_0\), then
  \(c_{(0)} a \in P^\pa\).

nodes.title: JoyceVertexAlgebra
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Section 1.4.}
  Joyce's vertex algebra for a smooth projective variety \(X\) is
  \[V_\bullet = \widehat{H}_\bullet(\mathcal{M}_X),\]
  a graded completion of the homology of the moduli stack \(\mathcal{M}_X\)
  parametrizing objects in \(D^b(X)\). The translation operator \(T\) comes from
  the \(B\mathbb{G}_m\)-action on \(\mathcal{M}_X\); the state-field correspondence
  \(Y(-,z)\) is defined using the direct-sum map
  \(\Sigma \colon \mathcal{M}_X \times \mathcal{M}_X \to \mathcal{M}_X\)
  and a perfect complex \(\Theta\) on \(\mathcal{M}_X \times \mathcal{M}_X\)
  encoding the obstruction theory.

  The associated Lie algebra is \(\widecheck{V}_\bullet = V_{\bullet+2}/TV_\bullet\).

  \textbf{Note}: This construction is not formalized in Mathlib; it appears as an
  assumption in the Lean files.

nodes.title: GrossIsomorphism
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Theorem 3.8 (corrected from Gross \cite{Gr19}).}
  Let \(X\) be a variety of class~D (curves, surfaces, rational 3-folds and rational
  4-folds; cf.\ the paper's Remark on class~D). Then Joyce's vertex algebra
  \(V_\bullet\) for \(X\) is naturally isomorphic (as a vertex algebra) to the lattice
  vertex algebra constructed from \((K^\bullet(X), K^0_\sst(X), \chi_\mathrm{sym})\),
  where
  \[\chi_\mathrm{sym}(v,w) = \int_X \mathrm{ch}(v^\vee)\, \mathrm{ch}(w)\, \tdX
    + \int_X \mathrm{ch}(w^\vee)\, \mathrm{ch}(v)\, \tdX.\]
  Under this isomorphism, \(H^\bullet(\mathcal{M}_X)\) is identified with \(\BD^X\).

  \textbf{Proof}: Sorry. This is Theorem~3.8 of the paper (with corrections to Gross \cite{Gr19}).
nodes.proof_tex:
Sorry in Lean. The proof uses Kac's lattice vertex algebra construction and an
  identification of Joyce's state-field correspondence with the bosonic and fermionic
  parts of the lattice VOA via the Hodge decomposition of \(K^\bullet(X) \cong H^\bullet(X)\).

nodes.title: ConformalElementPair
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Theorems 4.2 and 4.4.}
  Assume \(X\) satisfies Assumption~1.2. The pair vertex algebra
  \(V_\bullet^\pa = \widehat{H}_\bullet(\mathcal{P}_X) \cong V_\bullet \otimes V_\bullet\)
  admits a natural conformal element \(\omega \in V_4^\pa\) with central charge
  \(2\chi(X)\). The Virasoro operators \(\{L_n^\pa\}_{n \in \mathbb{Z}}\) induced by
  \(\omega\) on \(V_\bullet^\pa\) are \emph{dual} to the pair Virasoro operators
  \(\{\bL_n^\pa\}_{n \geq -1}\) on \(\Dpa\): for all \(D \in \Dpa\),
  \(a \in V_\bullet^\pa\),
  \[\langle \bL_n^\pa(D),\, a \rangle = \langle D,\, L_n^\pa(a) \rangle.\]

  \textbf{Proof}: Sorry. This is the content of Theorems~4.2 and~4.4 of the paper.
nodes.proof_tex:
Sorry in Lean. The conformal element \(\omega\) arises from Kac's fermionic
  construction applied to an isotropic splitting of
  \(K^1(X) = K^{\bullet,\bullet+1} \oplus K^{\bullet+1,\bullet}\) given by the
  Hodge decomposition (this is where Assumption~1.2 is used). Duality between
  \(L_n^\pa\) and \(\bL_n^\pa\) is Theorem~4.2; the equivalence to primary states
  is Theorem~4.4.

nodes.title: VirasoroConstraintsAsPrimaryStates
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Theorem~1.5 (= Theorem~4.4).}
  Assume \(X\) satisfies Assumption~1.2. Then:
  \begin{enumerate}
  \item \cref{conj:virasoro-sheaves-inv} is equivalent to
        \([M]^\vir \in \widecheck{V}_\bullet \subseteq \widecheck{V}_\bullet^\pa\)
        being a primary state in \(\widecheck{P}_0\).
  \item \cref{conj:virasoro-pairs} is equivalent to
        \([P]^\vir_{(q^*V, \mathbb{F})} \in V_\bullet^\pa\)
        being a primary state in \(P_0^\pa\).
  \end{enumerate}

  \textbf{Proof}: Sorry. This is Theorem~4.4 of the paper; the proof uses the duality
  \cref{thm:conformal-element-pair}.
nodes.proof_tex:
\uses{thm:conformal-element-pair}
  Sorry in Lean. The equivalence follows from the duality between \(L_n^\pa\) and
  \(\bL_n^\pa\): the condition \(\int_{[M]^\vir} \bL_n^\pa(D) = 0\) for all \(D\)
  translates via the pairing to the condition \(L_n^\pa([M]^\vir) = 0\) for all
  \(n \geq 1\), which is precisely the primary-state condition.

nodes.title: VirasoroConjectureInvariantClasses
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Conjecture (invariant-class Virasoro
  constraints, following Theorem~1.5).}
  Under the assumptions of \cref{thm:virasoro-primary}, the moduli spaces \(M\),
  possibly containing strictly semistable sheaves, satisfy the Virasoro constraints
  in the sense that the Joyce invariant class \([M]^\inva\) is a primary state:
  \[[M]^\inva \in \widecheck{P}_0 \subseteq \widecheck{V}_0^\pa.\]
  (When \(M\) contains no strictly semistable sheaves, \([M]^\inva = [M]^\vir\) and
  this recovers \cref{conj:virasoro-sheaves-inv} via \cref{thm:virasoro-primary}.)

nodes.title: VirasoroBradlowPairs
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Theorem~1.4.}
  The moduli spaces of Bradlow pairs \(P_\alpha^t\) satisfy the pair Virasoro
  constraints (\cref{conj:virasoro-pairs}) for every \(t > 0\) in the three settings
  of \cref{thm:main}, i.e.\ \((m,d) = (2,2)\), \((1,1)\), and \((m,d) = (2,1)\)
  provided Assumption~A holds.

  \textbf{Proof}: Sorry. This is Theorem~1.4 of the paper, proved together with
  \cref{thm:main} by rank reduction.
nodes.proof_tex:
Sorry in Lean. Proved by induction on \(\rk(\alpha)\): the limit moduli spaces
  \(P_\alpha^\infty\) satisfy \cref{conj:virasoro-pairs} directly, and the
  wall-crossing formula together with \cref{thm:wall-crossing} propagates the pair
  constraints to \(P_\alpha^t\) for all \(t > 0\).

nodes.title: MainTheoremVirasoroConstraints
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Theorem~1.1.}
  The Virasoro conjecture (\cref{conj:virasoro-sheaves-inv}) holds, when
  there are no strictly semistable sheaves, for:
  \begin{enumerate}
  \item The moduli spaces \(M_C(r,d)\) of slope-semistable bundles on any smooth
        projective curve \(C\).
  \item The moduli spaces \(M_S^H(r,c_1,c_2)\) of slope- or Gieseker-semistable
        torsion-free sheaves on any smooth projective surface \(S\) with
        \(h^{1,0}(S) = h^{2,0}(S) = 0\).
  \item (Conditional on Assumption~A) The moduli spaces \(M_S^H(\beta,n)\) of
        slope-semistable one-dimensional sheaves on any smooth projective surface
        \(S\) with \(h^{1,0}(S) = h^{2,0}(S) = 0\).
  \end{enumerate}
  \textbf{Proof sketch}: By induction on rank via wall-crossing
  (\cref{thm:wall-crossing}). For rank~1, the result is known (curves: symmetric
  powers; surfaces: Hilbert schemes, due to Maulik--Oblomkov--Okounkov--Pandharipande
  and Moreira). The induction step uses Bradlow pairs at the Joyce--Song wall,
  wall-crossing compatibility, and a projective bundle formula. Sorry in Lean.
nodes.proof_tex:
\uses{thm:wall-crossing, thm:virasoro-primary}
  Sorry in Lean. See Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Theorem~1.1 and Section~5.

nodes.title: PunctualQuotSchemeVirasoro
nodes.statement:
\textit{Source: Bojko--Lim--Moreira, Theorem~1.6.}
  Let \(X\) be a curve or a surface and let \(V\) be a torsion-free sheaf on \(X\).
  Then the punctual Quot scheme \(\mathrm{Quot}_X(V, n)\) satisfies the pair Virasoro
  constraints (\cref{conj:virasoro-pairs}).

  \textbf{Proof}: Sorry. Proved in the Appendix using Joyce--Song wall-crossing.
